Yep, this is expected behavior: `new.rambler.ru` is a perfectly valid path, too,
e.g. `http://google.com/new.rambler.ru`. It's ambiguous.

furl defaults to adopt that string as the path. This is the default behavior of
Python's `urlparse()`, too.

```python
>>> from urlparse import urlparse
>>> urlparse('new.rambler.ru')
ParseResult(scheme='', netloc='', path='new.rambler.ru', params='', query='', fragment='')
```

To tell furl to adopt `new.rambler.ru` as the host, you can explicitly set the
host

```python
>>> from furl import furl
>>> f = furl().set(host='new.rambler.ru')
>>> f.host
'new.rambler.ru'
```

or provide a scheme so furl knows that `new.rambler.ru` is a host, not a path.

```python
>>> from furl import furl
>>> f = furl('http://new.rambler.ru')
>>> f.host
'new.rambler.ru'
```

Does that answer your question?

Awesome. You found a bug. `set()` should replace and/or add to query parameters, not remove any.

I'll fix this bug shortly.

Until this bug is fixed, combine `remove()` and `add()`.

```python
>>> from furl import furl
>>> furl('http://www.a.de/?search=foo&page=1').remove(['page']).add({'page': 2}).url
'http://www.a.de/?search=foo&page=2'
```
Sounds reasonable. Are you thinking they would go in the README? Can you draft the change in a PR?

The solution to all of these problems is to use
```
ipdb> !list(('anything', 'at all'))
['anything', 'at all']
```
The `!` basically circumvents all internal debugger commands.

* `tenacity/doc/source/conf.py` is the Sphinx conf so it's not used as is, there are all false positives.
* `retry_always` is exported for library consumers
* All function/variable in tests are also used or defined that way to match the callback format.

So yeah, everything is false positive. Nice tool ;)

signalfd can be a bit counter intuitive as the signalfd has a separate signal mask to the normal unix signals and if you wish to have delivery only via signalfd you need to ensure that the appropriate signals are set to not fire via the standard mechanisms (signal module)

none of these will make a program more performant, but they may make it slightly 'cleaner' at the cost of moving the complexity to a 3rd party library, the timerfd however can be useful as an interval timer to time how long something has occurred for. Let's have a look at the "dpkt" source code.
There are a lot of classes that allows us to work with many type of the packets: TCP, UDP, HTTP, Radius and the like.
All of them inherits from dpkt.Packet class which actually determines byte order.
Here is the answer (from dpkt.py):
-----//------
class Packet(object):

    Base packet class, with metaclass magic to generate members from self.hdr.
    hdr should be defined as a list of (name, structfmt, default) tuples
    __byte_order__ can be set to override the default ('>')
-----//------
__byte_order__ field determines either big-endian or little-endian byte order should be used.
The default byte order for dpkt is set in _MetaPacket class line 37:
-----//------

    t.__hdr_fmt__ = getattr(t, 'byte_order', '>') + ''.join([x[1] for x in st])
-----//------
To sum up, __"dpkt" takes care about byte ordering__ and it uses big-endian (which is default network byte order) format as a default byte order.

Some more info could be found here:

    https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Endianness
    http://betterexplained.com/articles/understanding-big-and-little-endian-byte-order/
    Official python documentation: https://docs.python.org/2/library/struct.html#struct-alignment

Two datasets need their dimensionality reduced: 1) the original dataset (scatterplot) and 2) the function drawn with the current guess.

1) given x_hat  and y_hat as independent datapoints and z_hat as corresponding dependent data one can construct the probability of finding x and y p(x, y) by doing a kernel density estimation.
Next, \<x_hat\> needs to be made by binning x_hat. \<z_hat\> can then be made by averaging all x_hat, y_hat in each \<x_hat\> using p(x_hat, y_hat) as weights. Note that this needs to be normalized by sum(p(x_hat, y_hat)) for all x_hat, y_hat in \<x_hat\>.

2) Make a grid of x, y points, and evaluate your guessed function f(x, y) to find z. For plotting, calculate \<z\> for each x by averaging z, using p(x, y) as weights. Note that this needs to be normalized by p(x).

Hey @pohmelie,
 
I understand that the behavior is a bit confusing. It's an attempt to balance between expectations of the `addict` module. Today, whatever you give `addict` in the constructor will be recursively iterated over and any `dict` will be turned into a `Dict`. In contrast, whatever you set as an attribute on the `Dict` instance will _not_ be copied.  While this is the way we intended it to work, it can lead to surprises. 

At this point, I am leaning towards moving today's `__init__`-functionality in it's own static method, say `Dict.from_dict`, and let `__init__` do as you expect over. This would however warrant a major release.

Sure I know, I also do not like this solution so much in my own projects. Therefore, I by myself actually use the following solutions (although the second is kind of advanced):

1. Most of the time, I use the __init__ method for the "Parameters" section (sphinx can handle that if you for example set `autoclass_content = 'both'` in the `conf.py` for the [autodoc extension](http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/ext/autodoc.html#confval-autoclass_content).
   ```python
   class PBSCluster(JobQueueCluster):
       """Docstring of PBSCluster"""

       @d.dedent
       def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
           """
           Parameters
           ----------
           %(Params2use)s
           """
   ```
2. Where I however use it generally for all subclasses, I use it in a metaclass (I do that for example for my [Formatoption class](https://github.com/Chilipp/psyplot/blob/f0a161d6a7eb7d58c04b43a899410c889f097fa7/psyplot/plotter.py#L113))
   ```python
   import six

Most of `PyPI` is now converted to wheels, and in my limited interactions with binary wheels I have not faced any issues. There used to be some some [issues](https://github.com/pypa/pip/pull/1465), but mostly they are sorted out now.

There is no compile farm, the package owner compiles the binaries, and signs the package for whatever arch/OS they care :) the rest of users will get `sdist` and need to have the tools for local compilation.

Yes that's right. It's a horrible old-fashioned format. I think the designers were worried about having to keep an array of points in memory.
   One might be lucky and have each point referred to with identical coordinates in each triangle that it appears in, but very often this is not the case in STL found in the field, particularly STL generated by certain CAD programs.
 
Another nasty feature of this format is that it is so simple but lacking in a precise specification, so that it is usually generated howsoever. Like CSV. This demands flexibility of a reader. For example, the file sphere.stl distributed with the Gmsh sources foils the Python stl package that I referred to because its first line is

```
solid Created by Gmsh
```

It works if the second and third spaces are replaced by underscores. The [numpy-stl](http://numpy-stl.readthedocs.io/en/latest) package, on the other hand, doesn't mind. Some STL omits the name of the solid.

Other variations in compliance:
* The specification demands that the triangles between 'solid' and 'endsolid' form a closed surface (topological sphere) but many STL files are used to define surfaces with boundary loops, e.g. for boundary patches for snappyHexMesh.
* The specification wants exactly one 'solid', but many STL files contain several concatenated. Ditto.
* The specification wants everything in the first octant; i.e. all coordinates nonegative, but this is widely flouted.

These misfeatures defeat, as you say, many applications. The best thing a user could do would be to generate an alternative like GTS or OFF, but the format (again like CSV) stubbornly persists and the finite element analyst does often have to deal with it. On reflexion, I shouldn't have described reading it above as 'pretty easy'. 
   I don't think the stl or numpy-stl packages deal with the question of identifying coincident or nearly coincident points; they're not for finite element analysis.

So, after careful consideration and consultation of other devs, I've finalized the version of the API that will go public.

Rather than the long names, the functions are simply `ceil()` and `floor()` and they have the same API as the builtins, except they take an optional `options` argument.

The argument order was changed to make the target number first, in line with the existing `ceil` and `floor`. I think you'll agree that the `options` argument name is more semantic than the old `src` name. It also appropriately hints at the discretization effect it has on the mode of `ceil`/`floor`.

The `allow_equal` argument was eliminated because the built-in functions always allow equal. `ceil(4.0)` gives `4.0`. For the rare cases that require non-equal options only, this can be managed by removing the value from the options before calling the function.

The `sorted_src` argument was removed and sorting is always performed. Timsort is O(n) for sorted lists. I ran some tests and there would need to be many calls with many options to have a noticeable effect. While `sorted_src` felt like something I might use on the right day, it does not feel like something the standard library would have included. The more streamlined API makes these new functions feel like more powerful builtins instead of all-new functionality.

I've updated the architecture section of the docs to emphasize this preference for keeping APIs in the tone of the stdlib: https://boltons.readthedocs.org/en/latest/architecture.html#design-of-a-bolton

I've implemented a new matching strategy which also takes the http verb into account when selecting the policy. 

This is to counter the following problem:

We have 2 policies:

```
policy=theworld,ourdomain

ourdomain_origin=*.ourdomain.com
ourdomain_headers=*
ourdomain_methods= HEAD, OPTIONS, GET, POST, PUT, DELETE
ourdomain_maxage=180

theworld_origin=*
theworld_headers=*
theworld_methods=HEAD, OPTIONS, GET
theworld_maxage=180
```

When doing a preflight request originating from ourdomain (trying to do a PUT) theworld matches and is returned as the effective policy. PUT however isn't allowed in that policy. I've named the new matching strategy mostspecific, naming can probably be better though. 

No existing functionality is broken, it should behave as before for a given configuration.

As to the question why we don't invert the policies, i.e.  ```policy=ourdomain,theworld```, this has to do with the following bug: https://bugs.chromium.org/p/chromium/issues/detail?id=453306

Firefox still seems to be experiencing the problem.

This causes a GET  from a.ourdomain.com to the server being cached, including the Origin header. When doing a GET from b.ourdomain.com to the server ```Origin:a.ourdomain.com``` is returned which causes the request to fail. By switching the policies this problem effectively goes away but then we ran into the above problem which we are now trying to fix.

My primary goal for this request is to be able to hover over a line in a grafana graph and a tooltip will pop up with the full command line of the process I'm interested in.  How can this best be accomplished?

primary = PID: This would be a good choice if we can figure out how to get Grafana to tooltip a second field instead of the one we're querying on.  I'm not so interested in the PID per se, but it does offer the convenience of guaranteeing uniqueness for each process.

primary = Command Line: I think this would be the most straight-forward approach.  But these lines can get pretty long with lots of funny characters.  Can InfluxDB handle this?  Perhaps truncate at # of characters?  This approach also leaves the danger of a program like apache spawning multiple processes for the same thing... these wouldn't look unique in this case.

primary = PID-Command Line: Concatenating the PID and the command line might be the best approach to guarantee uniqueness if we can't figure out the tooltip shows secondary info problem.

primary = command: This does not get granular enough for me.  Think of the Apache web server, or in my case the Jenkins node allowing simultaneous runs. 

Thank you for considering this feature!

@gordthompson , many many thanks.  Your answer ended my hours of troubleshooting for my first pyodbc connect.  Everything worked, except pyodbc connection authentication, I should have suspected about the semicolon earlier in the process.

I can also confirm that this affects pyodbc 4.0.23 but not 4.0.22.

Explicitly encoding the string value as @veeology mentioned works for me, though I also need to change empty strings to `None` as @billmccord said — not really viable if you're hoping to preserve the distinction between empty strings and NULLs (I'm pushing data from a pyodbc MySQL cursor to a pyodbc SQL Server cursor).

However I've made two further observations:

- this only happens to columns declared with the `varchar(max)` and `nvarchar(max)` data types; you can confirm this by changing your `(max)` column length to 8000 or 4000 (or less) respectively
- HY010 doesn't happen if the _params_ value is a generator object, but definitely happens if it's a sequence; however I believe that fast_executemany behaviour doesn't occur with a generator/non-sequence as performance completely tanks when attempting to do so

My guess then is that 4.0.23 is no longer automatically preparing parameter values as binary for `(max)` columns during fast_executemany operations. This would be explained by my two observations here and @veeology's workaround, though the empty string thing confuses me.

I'm personally reverting to 4.0.22 as I have to write a fair bit of extra code to work around this and it incurs a performance penalty of about 25%. However if anybody does use @veeology's workaround for 4.0.23, I think you'll be wanting to encode in `utf_16_le` for `nvarchar(max)` columns and you're probably safest (codepage-wise) with `utf8` for `varchar(max)`, based on the docs [here](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/connect/odbc/linux-mac/programming-guidelines?view=sql-server-2017#character-set-support).

Tested and pushed the changes as 1.8.1.93. Extra workarounds were needed to make sure that if different timeframes are used the larger timeframes are not pulled too early (subday timeframes), because the *master* data is delivering and requesting the other datas to deliver.

As expected and with the *IB Demo* as reference, which is already a low liquidity provider, tests below `Seconds 15` as the timeframe have a tendency to de-synchronize the data (the 2nd data being the one with lower liquidity). Tests with `Seconds 30` and above have kept the data well synchronized in minutes long runs. `Seconds 15` seems to be the limit which sometimes runs well and sometimes doesn't, using `TWR` (data0) and `APPL` (data1)

In any case the design which was used for *backtesting* allowing easy synchronization of data has probably already been stretched beyond maximum and further modifications may be do more bad than good. Not a thought following this discussion, but an already existing one.

**NOTE**
You may use a trick to have the script running *5 minutes* and doing nothing even if the platform has already reported `data.LIVE`.

  - Add a simple moving average for control purposes.
  - Give it a period long enough to cover 5 minutes with the selected timeframe (example: period 10 for a timeframe of 30 seconds)
  - `next` in the strategy will be 1st called when the moving average produces meaningful data, in this case after 5 minutes.

I don't think this is possible. Programs like Microsoft word write files in an in-memory buffer. This has no interaction with the file system. When you "save" a file it either modifies or overwrites the old file with the content from the buffer. However, closing the buffer (the window) has no effect on the file system therefore cannot be detected. You're better off seeing if the program writing the file has a way for you to hook into it, and using an api call to run a method when the window closing.

Hi @losisli 

It seems it's no easy to "fully" include 115Browser functionalities since it's a pre-built Chrome-based browser with many built-in objects and routines that cannot be inspected. However, I do find a workaround to obtain the extra quota (using this API), along with a few code changes on some required parameters passed in via environment variables.

To make it work, you need to first install 115Browser and log in your account with it. Here are the steps:

1. Log in your account with 115Browser.
2. Open Developer Tools (https://developer.chrome.com/devtools/docs/shortcuts)
3. Navigate your page to **文件** - **离线下载** tab
4. Go to **Network** tab of the Developer Tools, find the request path that starts with `?ct=offline&ac=space`, click on it to show the **Headers** sub-tab, roll down to the bottom to show **Query String Parameters**. Here, we need the values of "sign" and "time". (see screenshot below)
    ![u115b](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5781687/8472380/9c5ac56e-2055-11e5-9c1f-faf6224eb7f8.png)

5. Copy the values in Step 4, and make them environment variables as below when invoking your program:
    ```
    U115_BROWSER_SIGN=value_of_sign
    U115_BROWSER_TIME=value_of_time
    ```

For example, on Unix you can do this:

```console
$ export U115_BROWSER_SIGN=value_of_sign
$ export U115_BROWSER_TIME=value_of_time
$ python your_code.py
```
or

```console
$ U115_BROWSER_SIGN=value_of_sign U115_BROWSER_TIME=value_of_time python your_code.py
```

In your code, you can check if this works, with code like this:

```python
from u115 import API

api = API()
api.login('username@example.com', 'password')
print(api.task_quota)
# Check if this correctly reflects your usage (100 minus used count), since
# api.task_total is not implemented at this time
```

I tested the above workaround on OS X 10.10. I believe it should work on other platforms, as long as they distribute the Chrome-based 115Browsers with same pre-built routines on those platforms.

It's not dirty, it's exactly the was django's transaction.atomic() is
supposed to be used.

HOWEVER, I didn't notice that you were using RevisionMixin! So that
requires some more explanation.

RevisionMixin only does not create a revision for GET, HEAD or OPTIONS
request methods, since those are supposed to be "safe", and not modify
data. You can get around this by using the reversion.create_revision()
decorator manually, or by using a more appropriate HTTP method.

import math
from sklearn import neighbors
import os
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score 
import os.path
import pickle
from PIL import Image, ImageDraw
import face_recognition
from face_recognition.face_recognition_cli import image_files_in_folder

ALLOWED_EXTENSIONS = {'png', 'jpg', 'jpeg'}


def train(train_dir, model_save_path=None, n_neighbors=None, knn_algo='ball_tree', verbose=False):
    """
    Trains a k-nearest neighbors classifier for face recognition.

    :param train_dir: directory that contains a sub-directory for each known person, with its name.

     (View in source code to see train_dir example tree structure)

     Structure:
        <train_dir>/
        ├── <person1>/
        │   ├── <somename1>.jpeg
        │   ├── <somename2>.jpeg
        │   ├── ...
        ├── <person2>/
        │   ├── <somename1>.jpeg
        │   └── <somename2>.jpeg
        └── ...

    :param model_save_path: (optional) path to save model on disk
    :param n_neighbors: (optional) number of neighbors to weigh in classification. Chosen automatically if not specified
    :param knn_algo: (optional) underlying data structure to support knn.default is ball_tree
    :param verbose: verbosity of training
    :return: returns knn classifier that was trained on the given data.
    """
    X = []
    y = []

    # Loop through each person in the training set
    for class_dir in os.listdir(train_dir):
        if not os.path.isdir(os.path.join(train_dir, class_dir)):
            continue

        # Loop through each training image for the current person
        for img_path in image_files_in_folder(os.path.join(train_dir, class_dir)):
            image = face_recognition.load_image_file(img_path)
            face_bounding_boxes = face_recognition.face_locations(image)

            if len(face_bounding_boxes) != 1:
                # If there are no people (or too many people) in a training image, skip the image.
                if verbose:
                    print("Image {} not suitable for training: {}".format(img_path, "Didn't find a face" if len(face_bounding_boxes) < 1 else "Found more than one face"))
            else:
                # Add face encoding for current image to the training set
                X.append(face_recognition.face_encodings(image, known_face_locations=face_bounding_boxes)[0])
                y.append(class_dir)

    # Determine how many neighbors to use for weighting in the KNN classifier
    if n_neighbors is None:
        n_neighbors = int(round(math.sqrt(len(X))))
        if verbose:
            print("Chose n_neighbors automatically:", n_neighbors)

    # Create and train the KNN classifier
    knn_clf = neighbors.KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=n_neighbors, algorithm=knn_algo, weights='distance')
    knn_clf.fit(X, y)

    # Save the trained KNN classifier
    if model_save_path is not None:
        with open(model_save_path, 'wb') as f:
            pickle.dump(knn_clf, f)

    return knn_clf


def predict(X_img_path, knn_clf=None, model_path=None, distance_threshold=0.6):
    """
    Recognizes faces in given image using a trained KNN classifier

    :param X_img_path: path to image to be recognized
    :param knn_clf: (optional) a knn classifier object. if not specified, model_save_path must be specified.
    :param model_path: (optional) path to a pickled knn classifier. if not specified, model_save_path must be knn_clf.
    :param distance_threshold: (optional) distance threshold for face classification. the larger it is, the more chance
           of mis-classifying an unknown person as a known one.
    :return: a list of names and face locations for the recognized faces in the image: [(name, bounding box), ...].
        For faces of unrecognized persons, the name 'unknown' will be returned.
    """
    if not os.path.isfile(X_img_path) or os.path.splitext(X_img_path)[1][1:] not in ALLOWED_EXTENSIONS:
        raise Exception("Invalid image path: {}".format(X_img_path))

    if knn_clf is None and model_path is None:
        raise Exception("Must supply knn classifier either thourgh knn_clf or model_path")

    # Load a trained KNN model (if one was passed in)
    if knn_clf is None:
        with open(model_path, 'rb') as f:
            knn_clf = pickle.load(f)

    # Load image file and find face locations
    X_img = face_recognition.load_image_file(X_img_path)
    X_face_locations = face_recognition.face_locations(X_img)

    # If no faces are found in the image, return an empty result.
    if len(X_face_locations) == 0:
        return []

    # Find encodings for faces in the test iamge
    faces_encodings = face_recognition.face_encodings(X_img, known_face_locations=X_face_locations)

    # Use the KNN model to find the best matches for the test face
    closest_distances = knn_clf.kneighbors(faces_encodings, n_neighbors=1)
    are_matches = [closest_distances[0][i][0] <= distance_threshold for i in range(len(X_face_locations))]

    # Predict classes and remove classifications that aren't within the threshold
    return [(pred)  for pred in zip(knn_clf.predict(faces_encodings))]


    """
    Shows the face recognition results visually.

    :param img_path: path to image to be recognized
    :param predictions: results of the predict function
    :return:
    """
    pil_image = Image.open(img_path).convert("RGB")
    draw = ImageDraw.Draw(pil_image)

    for name, (top, right, bottom, left) in predictions:
        # Draw a box around the face using the Pillow module
        draw.rectangle(((left, top), (right, bottom)), outline=(0, 0, 255))

        # There's a bug in Pillow where it blows up with non-UTF-8 text
        # when using the default bitmap font
        name = name.encode("UTF-8")

        # Draw a label with a name below the face
        text_width, text_height = draw.textsize(name)
        draw.rectangle(((left, bottom - text_height - 10), (right, bottom)), fill=(0, 0, 255), outline=(0, 0, 255))
        draw.text((left + 6, bottom - text_height - 5), name, fill=(255, 255, 255, 255))

    # Remove the drawing library from memory as per the Pillow docs
    del draw

    # Display the resulting image

Just pushed version 4.4.2 to PyPI.

I think the major breakdowns caused by the UI change have now all been resolved. Thanks again to @mcookhome and @johannfrias.

I expect many more minor bugs to be found in the coming weeks.

This UI change revealed several notable gaps in test coverage:
* We have no tests involving albums charts.
* We have no test for when a non-existent chart name is used: `billboard.ChartData('nonsense')`. We don't even have a specified behavior for this case.
* We had (until 8a46ef083380f481f15f46f7b53f6af95f4a9025) no tests for `previousDate` and `nextDate`.
* We need more tests involving date edge cases (earliest chart, most recent chart, etc.).

I will make a new issue for these.

> Suggesting charts is a neat idea, but I don't want to have to maintain a list of all existing charts.

It seems that [https://www.billboard.com/charts](https://www.billboard.com/charts) maintains all of the possible chart names at the bottom panels; ideally the names could be scrapped and loaded upon importing billboard.py (this does exclude year-end charts). And there could be a function to print the possible chart names (but there seems to be a lot of them) (but it could also be printed in an organized manner).

As to suggesting charts, I have an idea that doesn't take much work if you don't mind copying verbatim from StackOverflow. It involves using Levenshtein distance, which measures similarity between two strings; I could have a function that returns the chart name yielding the minimum distance between it and the user input.

> We need more tests involving date edge cases (earliest chart, most recent chart, etc.).

I played with this quickly, and I came up with 3 potential cases:
1. Actual dates on or preceding the date of a chart's current week will default to either that date's chart or the next issue after that date (as expected)
2. Actual dates strictly after the date of a chart's current week will be a successful url request but won't load any song data (raises `billboard.BillboardParseException`)
   * (Not sure if this is the best solution but) Could be fixed at `fetchEntries` level, at line containing `dateElement = ...` where the current date on the webpage can be compared against the date in the url
3. Not actual dates (ex. 2018-32-32) will raise `billboard.BillboardNotFoundException` since it's a bad url.
   * Could be fixed at `__init__` level perhaps with datetime validation


(Also note that these are merely assumptions from fast observations, I could be partially mistaken.)

@Kristinita, I've read over all your links that you posted describing why `style` shouldn't be used. In general, they've covered extremely general cases, and while I generally agree, I disagree with it in the context of Progressbar, let me explain why. I'm open to discussing this further, but I'll lay out my argument.

My personal opinion is that just because something is generally considered bad, does not make it universally bad.  I understand the argument that having `style` outside of CSS is generally bad, and I agree.  But that does **not** mean `style` is **always** bad.  In this case, we are binding the width to a progress bar via `style`.   The `width` provided in `style` is **only** valid for **that** instance of a progress bar.  It is the only thing we write to `style`. 5 progress bars in a class `percent10` would not necessarily have the same width. For general styling we provide a class. The class covers progress bars that fall within a range of widths. The class is meant for general styling such as colors which absolutely should not be in `style`.

You could use JavaScript to set the widths, but now you are dependent on JavaScript for something so basic.  If people have JavaScript disabled, or it doesn't load etc., the progress bar will still render properly by using `style`.  While you could generate a CSS file with 100 increments to cover all percentages, I feel this is a bad use of time.

Us providing the width of a progress bar in `style` is not a maintainability concern (as described in the provided links) as the width is auto-generated.  The developer is not meant to edit those widths in the HTML.

 If you feel that you are unwilling to add an exception to your linter, you can  use [`StripHtml`](https://facelessuser.github.io/pymdown-extensions/extensions/striphtml/) to strip all `style` attributes out of your documents. If your linter won't allow `style`, and you want to enforce this rule, then I see no problem with simply stripping `style` globally from auto-generated HTML from Markdown.

If one day CSS allows you to read an attribute of an element to calculate something like width (and it is a day when most browsers properly support it), then I'd consider removing `style`.

Do you still think there is a reason to include such a feature?  If so does StripHtml not suite your needs?

In my opinion, if it's something  we want to avoid, at least a warning is necessary (using logging package).

Consider a (not so expert or distracted) user who wants to use the mfcc function with a greater sampling rate than 16kHz, say 22kHz (a typical value), leaving all the other default parameters as they are. In this case, a 25ms frame is long 550 samples, so the frames will be cropped yielding to suboptimal results. The user will be totally unaware of this cropping behaviour and I don't think the user expects to mess up something just changing the sampling rate or the window size (aka frame size). 

[Side question: what parameters would you suggest in this case, given that 550 is only a little bigger than 512?]

Furthermore, not all the users are going to do cross validation on the nftt, rather many will use some typical set of parameters found in some paper, which usually provides sampling rate, window size and step (in ms), and number of coefficients.

@pbauer the problem is that the webdav PUT methods for both the File and Image types call read on the uploaded file and pass the resulting string into the constructor for either NamedBlobFile or NamedBlobImage. It's loading the entire contents of the file into memory and defeating the purpose of using blobs. So the change here just passes the uploaded file object into the constructor for NamedBlobFile or NamedBlobImage. The constructor will do the right thing as long as there is a plone.namedfile.interfaces.IStorage utility defined for whatever is being passed in. In this case what is being passed in is an instance of plone.app.blob.monkey.TemporaryFileWrapper.

to_decrypt = pgpy.PGPMessage.from_file(filename)

I wouldn't be too worried about keyword docs growing too big. Having all docs in one place has its benefits.

That said, it might be a good idea to have larger examples, especially when the example involves multiple keywords, outside the keyword docs. In that case I'd at least initially keep all such examples in one reST file. Different examples could be split into separate sections, table of contents added to the top, etc. If this file would then grow too large to maintain, the reST source code could be split and end result combined into a single document. At that point it would probably be a good idea to start using [Sphinx](http://www.sphinx-doc.org) and host docs at [Read the Docs](https://readthedocs.org/). After further investigation, this seems to be (partially) a race condition issue. If I introduce a delay before installing the VCS dependencies, or use `--sequential`, the correct version of django is installed.

The other half of this is that VCS dependencies (unlike any other dependency) do not use `--no-deps`, which makes the race condition possible.

So yeah - workaround is: use `--sequential` or run install multiple times.

I found more things:

- [Example Pipenv Upgrade Workflow](https://docs.pipenv.org/basics/#example-pipenv-upgrade-workflow) says the correct way to upgrade is the command `pipenv update --outdated`. But with the best-practice to specify no version in the Pipefile (just an asterix) `pip install` will also update all outdated packages.

- [Basic Concepts](https://docs.pipenv.org/#basic-concepts) says _"A virtualenv will automatically be created, when one doesn’t exist."_ and then goes to say _"To initialize a Python 3 virtual environment, run $ pipenv --three"_. This immediately makes one wonder why it says I nee to issue `pipenv --three` as a basic concept, when I actually never really do that? I tried it, and what it does is to re-create my pipenv with the version (three or two) specified if I already have one, which I will have after doing my first `pipenv install`. To be clear, the equivalent of:

```python
  newData = SynapseData(data.segment,   <--------------------   HERE
                          data.presynapticCell,
                          permanence)
    self._synapses[synapse] = newData
```

Would be more like:

```python
  self._synapses[synapse].segment = segment
  self._synapses[synapse].presynapticCell = presynapticCell
  self._synapses[synapse].permanence = permanence
```

Compared to creating a new instance, that's 3x additional lookup in `self._synapses` that wouldn't happen if you just created a new instance.  Otherwise, the attr lookup for `segment`, `presynapticCell`, `permanence` and assignment are equivalent to `SynapseData.__init__()`.

You don't have to take my word for it, though.  Feel free to profile it.  You might be right and it may be faster, but if it is, I doubt it will be by much.  If I'm wrong, great!

Using your example, if we can drop `segment` and `presynapticCell`, that would speed things up, too, but the difference between creating a new instance and assigning the value ought to be negligible.

@Allan-Nava your question is very confusing. Please re-edit it so that we could help you.

The JWT can have a list of audiences or a single string in the aud claim but the application can only specify a single audience in the `jwt.decode(token, audience=aud)` parameter.

This is in line with the spec which says
> Each principal intended to process the JWT MUST identify itself with a value in the audience claim.

The key distinction here being *a value*; not *one or more values*. This is consistent with the Ruby library as well.

I think the problem here is that in numexpr the locals dict is getting attached to the parent frame (the frame that calls `evaluate()`) instead of the current frame, as in your (and my) examples.  So that frame has to leave in order for the references to be cleared.

But as @pkgw and I discussed offline, it really ought to be safe to call `clear()` on a pure-Python dict; it seems implausible that it would corrupt CPython state.  So the fix would be to call `local_dict.clear()` at the end of [this function](https://github.com/pydata/numexpr/blob/master/numexpr/necompiler.py#L724-L739) (unless `local_dict` was passed explicitly).

If we agree this is the right fix, I'm happy to submit a PR.

@KostyaEsmukov Before implementing your suggestions, it might be worth stepping back to see if there's a simpler approach...

The problem arises because the Vincenty method fails to converge for nearly antipodal points.  For a long time, this was excused because computing such distances was rare.  However, it has real negative consequences.  For example, computing the distance matrix for 1000 randomly selected points results in about 8 failures of the Vincenty algorithm.  This means that the triangle inequality is not satisfied which, in turn, precludes the use of algorithms such as the VP tree for computing, e.g., the nearest port for a ship at sea.  See my [answer to a question on stackoverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45440700/calculate-distance-to-shore-or-coastline-for-a-vessel/45480555#45480555).

In 2009 I provided improved algorithms for computing geodesics.  This is more accurate (1e-8 m instead of 1e-4 m), but, more importantly, the distance calculation always converges.  The initial implementation was in C++; however, I provided a Python implementation in 2011.  This pull request provides hooks to this implementation.

Unfortunately, along the way the pull request became successively more convoluted:

* it retains the Vincenty algorithm;
* my native Python implementation is used if available;
* except that, if pyproj is available, that is used instead.

(pyproj uses a C implementation of my algorithms.)  This plethora of choices is pretty much guaranteed to cause confusion on the part of end users.

Here's my recommendation:

Replace Vincenty with my algorithms.  Either make geographiclib a dependency of geopy or just include the code as part of geopy.  (This approach has worked well for proj.4 which includes the C implementation. It helps that the source files are kept consistent so that changes in one library can be quickly brought into the other.)

This would also allow immediate access to routines for computing the area of geodesic polygons and for computing differential properties of geodesics (e.g., how much does the end point of a geodesic point move when the initial azimuth is changed by a small amount) which are useful, e.g., in doing adjustments of triangulation networks.

The potential push back is that my algorithms are 7x (resp. 4x) slower than the Vincenty distance (resp. destination).  I would say "ignore this".  Most casual users won't care (the distance calculation takes 0.3 ms).  Hard-core users can now use much better algorithms for distances involving many points because the triangle inequality is satisfied.

In this scheme, the routines with "vincenty" in their names would be deprecated and would merely call the corresponding routines with "vincenty" replaced by "geodesic".

Various options are available if speed really is an issue:

* hook up to pyproj
* provide a numpy implementation
* use Cython to call the C++ library

However, I regard this as secondary to getting decent algorithms into the hands of your users.

Machine specific configuration is not really exposed via API as it is, but there is an exception for tags.
```bash
$ curl -s localhost:8008/patroni | jq .
{
  "database_system_identifier": "6441030698134042663",
  "postmaster_start_time": "2017-07-10 08:48:44.272 CEST",
  "tags": {
    "nofailover": true,
    "noloadbalance": true,
    "nosync": true,
    "clonefrom": true
  },
  "xlog": {
    "location": 24094344
  },
  "patroni": {
    "scope": "batman",
    "version": "1.2.5"
  },
  "state": "running",
  "role": "master",
  "server_version": 90603
}
```

Currently it's not possible to change machine specific configuration via REST API.

Everything you see via `/config` endpoint is a global configuration stored in DCS. Therefore if you are trying to change it by doing POST or PATCH request to `/config`, you are changing it for entire cluster.

@seafoodbuffet,

by the way, Patroni can use some of the existing replicas to take basebackup from.

Those replicas should be `tagged` with `clonefrom` tag:
```yaml
tags:
  clonefrom: true
```

And Patroni will prefer to use such replicas instead of master.

In order to enable Kubernetes api for leader election you should set `DCS_ENABLE_KUBERNETES_API` env. By default it is using `Endpoints`. It eliminate a lot of problems and race conditions, because `subsets` for the leader endpoint are set at the same time when leader lock is acquired/updated. If you want to use config maps for leader election, you have to set `KUBERNETES_USE_CONFIGMAP` env. Basically all "magic" of generating patroni config yaml is hidden here: https://github.com/zalando/spilo/pull/198/files#diff-9ec119124414a288bc2f1edd468dcce5R399 and here: https://github.com/zalando/spilo/pull/198/files#diff-9ec119124414a288bc2f1edd468dcce5R442

And regarding the second one and client-based ttl.

This implementation can tolerate arbitrary clock difference on different hosts.
Nodes doesn't pay much attention on timestamps stored in annotations, they just check whether entire annotation has changed or not. If it didn't changed during TTL, it considered to be "stale".

Since we have TTL=30 and loop_wait=10, clock on one node should run 3 times faster than on the node where the master run to hit such race condition.

Transaction history
============

The internal an human transaction history is saved in the EVM world state. 
```
transactions :=  All transactions issued so far
human_transactions := All transactions initiated by a human (depth 0 transactions)
last_transaction := Last transaction in the list
last_human_transaction := Last human transaction in the list
current_transaction := Current unfinished transaction
```
Detectors and plugins can now browse the transaction history. A transaction can be of different types: CREATE, CALL and DELEGATECALL (so far). A transaction has: address, price, data, caller, value and gas.

When a transaction is initiated a unfinished transaction is added to the transaction lists.
Detectors can read the CALLDATA and VALUE used to start the current transaction and all the internal transactions involved.

Once the transaction is finished the result and return_data is set.

A result can be TXERROR, REVERT, STOP, THROW and SELFDESTRUCT. Some results can have a return_data associated.

> It would be nice if this library incorporated some directory listing / stat abilities of the old library

I think we can probably help with this but need a bit more detail. The basic gist here is "GCS technically does *not* support folders, and a library that did that was 'faking it' for you."

The "faking" is effectively:

- A path is a folder if it ends in `/`
- You can `ls` anything (worst case you'll get nothing back)
- `ls`'ing a directory is basically a prefix search (give me everything that starts with "/foo/bar/")
- `ls`'ing via prefix actually includes *subdirectories* as well! (ie, /foo/bar/baz/file.txt will be in the `ls` for /foo/bar)
 - If you want *just* the immediate directory, you need to exclude anything containing more slashes (I forgot how to do this one, I think there's a separator attribute?)

So -- if you give us some more detail about what exactly we're shooting for here, maybe we can figure out what methods make sense and how to fake this folder stuff in a consistent way (it'd be bad if we did it a way that wasn't quite right).

Hi @lukesneeringer 

Thanks for the response! In versions of the `google-cloud-pubsub` library bellow 0.28, the `subscription.pull()` method returns a list of (ack_id, messages)-tuples. We are setting the `max_messages` of that method to 1000, and consequently for most of our pipelines the list returned contains in between 100/200 messages.  Instead of processing each message separately, we are parsing all messages in that list at once, so that we can e.g. perform all required database updates in a single query, instead of having to perform a query for each of the messages. As the `google-cloud-pubsub` library version 0.28 works with callbacks on a message level, this seems a lot harder to do. So for our use-case it would be useful to have callbacks for a list of messages, instead for a single message.

I hope this clarifies my question!

@MichaelCurrie: `pathos` has been around since before python 3.x.  Most of it has been converted to python 3, however the portion that enables ssh-tunneling and ssh pipes has not been converted yet.  So, `pathos` is not yet a python 3 package.

However, there is good news.  If you are looking to use `pathos.multiprocessing` in python 3, you can.  You will only need the `multiprocess` package, which is what is underneath `pathos.multiprocessing`.  With it, you can pickle lambdas and other objects in parallel by leveraging `dill`.

This was introduced in [DFP v201802 release](https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/commit/a1340161bb5f42a9aba95db750fce3dfa81939ed#diff-af73cc83111e8be75d8d571321643be6).
Before, the logging message was always decoded from `bytes` to `str`/`unicode`, while after the change it's assumed that the decoding is only required in Python 2.
The logged SOAP responses from suds are still `bytes` in Python 3, however, which is why the `TypeError` is raised.

Yes, it will break backward compatibility, but also will clean up a lot of quirks dialects
I only applied it for jetty to not break other code, most others webservices use the other "dialect" (.net), for more info you could see the related project:

So I took a quick look and added a working example of Trinicon in [`bss/tests/test_trinicon.py`](https://github.com/LCAV/pyroomacoustics/blob/master/pyroomacoustics/bss/tests/test_trinicon.py). I have also improved Trinicon API so that you can specify all the parameters of the algorithm.

The current example I have successfully separates two speakers using two microphones. First, I have used anechoic conditions were separation is guaranteed to be possible. Second, the algorithm needs to be run several times on the speech sample in order to get convergence of the filters. Third, the tuning of the parameters seems finicky. Fourth, it is important that the signals to separate be broadband (I had first used harmonic singing signal which could not be successfully be separated).

So it seems that the algorithm is working, but might be hard to tune. Also, be careful if using only two mics, some source placements do not allow separation (e.g. if the sources are placed symmetrically across the line formed by the two microphones).

Please let me know if that helps and if you find other problems. If not I'll close this.

that actually wasn't the problem (well, it was *a* problem but not THE problem).

the problem was that pyhocon deals with backslashes in regexes by doubling them on the way in (which is wrong behavior) but json.dumps writes them out as-is, so that if you do a round-trip of a string that contains any number of backslashes, you end up with extra backslashes, which is what happened to us.

By the time we have compatible packages, the installer issues you enumerated will have long been resolved.  Specifically,

> URL to bash is hardcoded

The installer has already been converted to posix-compliant shell.

> licence count with OpenSSL, but LibreSSL is used on OpenBSD

Irrelevant.  Most conda environments link against their own ssl flavor.

> md5sum is expected, but md5 is available. FYI MD5 is not safe anymore, please switch to sha256

In conda, md5 is only used as a check of download integrity.  The use of md5 is NOT meant to be a security feature.  For the full flow of package delivery, sha256 is only marginally more "secure," if you're using that hash as a security feature.

It's my emphatic hope that we'll be implementing TUF package signing in the near future.

Testing multiple shells was a huge pain with the loop structure.  I replaced it with py.test parametrization.

To run specific tests, do something like this:

    <full path to py.test.exe> --shell="cmd.exe" tests/test_activate.py

or for more than one shell:

    <full path to py.test.exe> --shell="cmd.exe" --shell="msys" tests/test_activate.py

or for a specific test with a specific shell:

    <full path to py.test.exe> --shell="cmd.exe" tests/test_activate.py::test_activate_test1[cmd.exe]

That last one could probably be improved.  Presently, the default shell setting is [] (no tests at all, --shell required).  The defaults could be set to a list of string shell names.  However, when I've set that as the default, it seems that passing --shell does not override it, and I get all shells run.  Seems to be related to https://bugs.python.org/issue16399

I think the parcel conform information should be stored separately from the current `conform` object in the source JSON blobs. I see two options:

1. we include a `parcel_conform` object in the existing source document. This allows us to maintain a single record for license, name, coverage, etc. information

The underlying issue here is a real memory leak:
We have a very simple service proxying requests: we receive GET/PUT/POST request on an aiohttp server (basic HTTP server), and we make GET/POST/HEAD requests to another backend using the client part of aiohttp (these requests are SSL).

This basic proxy process is leaking around 300 MB of RAM per *hour* (and this process receives around 1 request per second, and makes 1-2 requests per second to the backend). Currently we're restarting the process regularly to avoid killing our servers…
It looks like the problem appeared with aiohttp 2, but i'm not entirely sure when. I'm having trouble replicating the issue locally so bisecting will be painful…

I'll continue investigating the issue, but i don't think the connection pooling or shutdown procedures are part of the issue here: the requests are made relatively slowly, with low concurrency (usually 1, at most 3-5)

--

Also, the problem is really specific to HEAD requests, which is very weird i guess. But the exact same script with GET requests does *not* leak anything:
If I leave cleanup_closed_disabled at False, with HEAD requests, the memory usage increase by around 30ko for each request during the first 3,000 requests (after that it becomes erratic).
If I do the exact same (cleanup_closed_disabled = False) but with GET requests instead, the memory usage is perfectly stable and not increase at all with each request.

We already have the `;` to suppress output like Matlab, but it only works if Python would have displayed output otherwise. Semicolons are not part of the AST, so we have some rather awkward logic to check for them - and that can't really be reused for what you want, because it only checks after Python tries to display a result.

I think we should set quite a high bar for any new special syntax we come up with - the code to deal with it is always tricky, and it risks confusing people about what Python syntax is (we turned off autocall by default for this reason).

Hi Matt,
THanks for the quick response and the explanation. I guess if you don't want to default to "unlimited" the only other non-surprising behavior is to have no default at all and require users to explicitly specify the max_tries. That would be very reasonable too. Throwing away messages quietly is a very strong action on a part of a library. This really should be an application level decision. In our case it lead to serious data loss. We have  a processing queue that if a message fails to process we divert it to "retry" queue where we periodically try to process all messages and if processing fails messages are requeued back to the "retry" queue. This way, transient problems don't affect the main work queue, but we don't lose messages and can process them later or examine it. If the depth of the "retry" queue grows too much we take a look and either fix the issues that cause messages to fail or discard them. The unexpected 5 tries caused us to lose many messages from the "retry" queue. We had some problem that we know we had to fix, but wasn't urgent. Suddenly the "retry" queue drained completely.

On Tue, Jul 11, 2017 at 6:15 PM, Adam Novak <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> In a lot of workflows in toil-vg, the main function kicks off a job and
> feeds it a bunch of arguments, and that job does some work (like building
> an index) and then kicks off something to use that index as a follow-on.
> Then that follow-on will do some more work and then kick off another
> follow-on to do some other task, and so on.
>
> This isn't the right way to structure things. Rather, each Toil job that
> actually does some work should just operate on its arguments and return its
> result. The task of orchestrating these smaller jobs into whole workflows
> should be accomplished by higher-level jobs that just kick off and string
> together child jobs.
>
> Structuring it this way makes the individual jobs more re-usable, because
> they only do one thing (like build some indexes) instead of, when you
> launch them, doing the entire remainder of the workflow.
>
> It also makes it easier to change the workflow, because you can just add
> more children to the orchestration jobs, rather than having to insert your
> additional work in a long chain of follow-ons that call each other.
>
> I'm addressing some of this in #238
> <https://github.com/BD2KGenomics/toil-vg/issues/238>, but I don't know if
> I will be able to get all of it right away.

This happens because `dateutil` tries to make some context-dependent guesses about what time zone you mean when the time zone is ambiguous. `GMT` could be `Europe/London` or `UTC`, and apparently it prioritizes `local()` over any other interpretation. I personally don't really like this implicit context, but it's what we have.

A somewhat related bug is #320. I think both can be fixed with a "sanity check" when assigning time zones to parsed dates, where, when you are pulling something that's supposed to be a `tzname()` output, you make sure that that `tzname()` *actually* applies at the time of year you've parsed.

Currently I think the best workaround would be to replace "GMT" with "UTC", which is hopefully unambiguous.

Thanks @andrewgiessel for your feedback.

> I like element in analogy with XML.

The name `Element` is definitely adopted.

> Why do some of these examples have a Figure as the top level element, but the first example uses map?

In my mind, a `Figure` is a container in which you can put several elements.
A `Map` is more specifically a *Leaflet* object into that container.
Doing the distinction between the two enables a clearer code, and extra features. For example:
* you would be able to put several maps in the same figure (I plan to compute this soon, based on something like `fig.add_subplot` in matplotlib).
* you can create a figure hosting a `Vega` graph, rather than a `Map`
* you can inherit from `Figure` with another template if you want you map to be displayed into a specific webpage.
* ...

In the first example, I've just created a shortcut for the user : when you call `my_map._repr_html_()`, then we check that the map's parent is a `Figure` (eventually create one), and call `my_map._parent._repr_html_()` so that the map in anyway represented into a figure.

> Now for some API nitpicking :grin: I was a bit surprised at child.add_to(parent). ...

You tip is really good. There's something to improve here. Today, `add_children` adds only one child in a row, but accept arguments `name` (for specifying the key in the children `OrderedDict`) and `index` (for specifying the position in the children `OrderedDict`). These arguments are important for the core machinery.
A few (disorganized) suggestions :

    m.add(A=childA, B=childB)                    # Handle name, but not the position
    m.add(childA, name='A').add(childB, name=B)  # Handle name and position, but no iterator
    m.iter_add([childA,childB])                  # Specific for iterations

@ahundt and @uatach : You are interested in a full example on how to use MongoDB in the docs, right? 
This should not be hard to do. 
1. On Ubuntu `sudo apt install mongo` installs a local MongoDB (though official docs recommend using their [mongodb-org](https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/tutorial/install-mongodb-on-ubuntu/) package).
You might need to manually start the demon with `sudo service mongodb start` (not sure).
Of course the docker based setup should also work, but might require some network configuration and later specifying the url in the mongoobserver. 
You can verify if it is running correctly by starting the mongo client with `mongo`.


2. With that in place you can just use `-m my_sacred_db_name` on your experiment to add a `MongoObserver` that writes to that database. 
Make sure to have the `pymongo` package installed (`pip install pymongo`)

3. You can use the mongo client to look at stored results. 
```
$ mongo
MongoDB shell version v3.4.7
connecting to: mongodb://127.0.0.1:27017
MongoDB server version: 3.4.7
> show dbs
admin   0.000GB
local   0.000GB
my_sacred_db_name
> use my_sacred_db_name
> show collections
fs.chunks
fs.files
runs
> db.runs.findOne()
{
	"_id" : 1,
	"experiment" : {
		"name" : "hello_cs",
		"base_dir" : "/home/greff/programming/sacred/examples",
....
```
But maybe a nicer way is to query from python using pymongo:
``` python
import pymongo
client = pymongo.MongoClient()
runs = client.my_sacred_db_name.runs
entry = runs.find_one()
```
Which returns a dictionary with the stored results for a run. The mongo interface has rich capacities for querying, to find the right experiment entry. 

4. Calling the experiment with the retrieved config:
``` python 
from my_experiment import ex
ex.run(config_updates=entry['config'])
```

Got it. The heuristic algorithms are in line with standard techniques for finding maximal satisfying subsets. However, I would also consider the facilities that let you assert with assumptions. 
Assumptions are temporary assertions that are retraced after search. They can be used instead of push/pop. The main tradeoff is as follows:
1. assumptions have to be propositional variables or negations of propositional variables. They cannot be compound expressions. To encode a compound formula F you add an assertion (a <=> F), and then assume a. 
2. Auxiliary clauses that are independent of assumptions and accumulated during search are retained. This can save on incremental calls. Push/pop only saves on pre-processing.

You can start out as follows:

            assert qij => cij  for each i, j
            assert pi <=> \/ qij  for each i

Then you call 

             check-with-assumptions p1, ..., pn

If the result is unsat, it means that it is not possible to cover all sets simultaneously.
The call returns an unsat core (not necessarily minimal), which you can use to trim the assumptions.
For example, assume that p1 is in the core, then you can perform a next call of the form:

             check-with-assumptions p2, p3, ..., pn

Eventually an assignment produces some subset of qij. Call this subset M (it corresponds to a model).

           assert \/_{qij in M} !qij  

Make another call:

            check-with-assumptions p1, ..., pn

The next model will include some qij which was not part of the previous maximal solution.

You can even break symmetries by adding some uncovered qij to the initial assumptions, but
obviously you don't want to remove qij from any cores you may encounter.

The algorithm so far accumulates a set of cores, trims them and throws them away after use.
It is possible to be more clever about the cores by relaxing the problem.
Suppose that p1, p2, p3, p4 is a core. So let us look for a solution where we are allowed to satisfy 3 of these 4 constraints. One encoding is as follows:

             p1' <=> p1 \/ p2
             p2' <=> p3 \/ (p1 & p2)
             p3' <=> p4 \/ ((p1 & p2) & p3)

which expands into:

             p1' <=> p1 \/ p2
             r1 <=> (p1 & p2)
             p2' <=> p3 \/ r1
             r2 <=> (r1 & p3)
             p3' <=> p4 \/ r2

Suppose that there is a solution to p1', p2', p3'. It is easy to see that it corresponds to a solution that satisfies 3 of the 4 constraints (this is in a nutshell the encoding suggested by Narodytska and Bacchus in AAAI 2014, used in the Eva solver and also used in Z3's maxsat implementation, see the IJCAI 2015 paper on my home page).

The next sat call is then

          check-with-assumptions p1', p2', p3', p5, p6, .., pn      

If you use the "trick" of adding a qij as an assumption you have to be careful with this transformation:
If qij is part of the core, you want to relax the other predicates but not qij. However, when you are trying to find a model that includes a different q_kl, then the relaxation should be discarded. 
So it is really not clear if the relaxation trick is preferable when the core includes qij, I would try the two versions. 

You could of course try an algorithm with assumptions that iterates over the qij (similar to your algorithms that maintain queues of uncovered cij).

`InverseLayer`?

> But then how would you change their values during training? I might not want to perform gradient updates on a given variable, but that doesn't mean I don't want to change it at all :)

Oh, right.

>> People can always subclass a layer to accomplish more complex parameterization.

> Agreed, I could live with that.

If `T.constant` is not an option and subclassing is too much effort, people can also easily exclude a variable from training by removing it from the `params` list before passing it to `lasagne.updates.something`. That's not too difficult either, actually. They could even use the `SharedVariable.tag` property for that, without us having to dirty our hands ;)

> I think we need to reflect on nomenclature here. If we are going to refer to both real and virtual parameters as 'params', we are going to hopelessly confuse people.

Yes... my idea was that only very few people would need to deal with that. Most people won't use the feature, and the ones that do `DenseLayer(..., W=otherlayer.W.T)` probably wouldn't think about what `get_all_params()` does if it just continues to work.

> Referring to both of these as 'params' is a bad idea. Maybe we should call the latter 'variables' or something? That's not really satisfactory either I guess. 'real params' and 'virtual params' is too cumbersome, but this terminology will do nicely for this discussion at least.

Referring to the latter as `variables` would mean we'd need to change a lot of documentation and function names (`get_all_variables()`, `set_all_variable_values()`) and be explicit about the distinction everywhere. That'd be very much against our fifth design goal. I'd try to avoid distinguishing the two as much as possible, to not confuse users who don't use the feature at all. That is, I'd continue to refer to everything as "parameters", and only where needed, add a note about what happens in the special case of a parameter being a "parameter expression".

> I agree that letting `get_all_params()` return the variables is the easiest and most straightforward thing to do, but I can't get behind a situation where `Layer.params` potentially contains expressions, and `get_all_params()` only returns shared variables.

I think that's totally fine if `get_all_params()` is documented to do that in the special case of `Layer.params` containing parameter expressions. All uses of `get_all_params()` in the library expect it to return shared variables. And again, we could give it a boolean flag that tells whether to unwrap parameter expressions or not, in case any user actually wants to obtain the parameter expressions for some reason (but what would be a use case? note that we couldn't even guarantee that it's free of duplicates in this case).

> I feel like that MNIST loading code doesn't belong in 'snippets' though... I see that more as a repository for library extensions (new layers, update functions, etc.), data loading boilerplate doesn't really belong there imo.

I think it could be both. We could have yet another subdirectory `shared` for things shared between examples (like the MNIST loading code), but we will probably have some of the `snippets` ending up in `tutorials` or `papers`, so we could just as well combine this into one.

> So is the plan still to rewrite the `mnist.py` examples as ipython notebooks?

As Sander said, not for the Lasagne repository, but possibly for the Recipes repository.

When these functions are called ąs I see `cc_dep1` and `cc_dep2` is always the default arch size since it is extended to that size in VEX, and only the operation indicates how many bits to use from the variable. For example:
```
   01 | t2 = GET:I16(esi)
   02 | PUT(cc_op) = 0x00000005
   03 | t3 = 16Uto32(t2)
   04 | PUT(cc_dep1) = t3
   05 | PUT(cc_dep2) = 0x0000007f
   06 | PUT(cc_ndep) = 0x00000000
   07 | PUT(eip) = 0x00000004
```

So in the above examples, I would do something like this before calling the functions:
```
    cc_dep1_nbits = cc_dep1[nbits-1:0]
    cc_dep2_nbits = cc_dep2[nbits-1:0]

    funcname = "pc_actions_%s_%s" % (op, cond)
    if funcname in globals():
        r = globals()[funcname](state, cc_dep1_nbits, cc_dep2_nbits, cc_ndep)
```

**IMO, the only question is that are these expressions equivalent for claripy backends (also in terms of complexity to solve them)**:
```
state.se.ULE(arg_l, arg_r)
vs 
state.se.ULE(arg_l - arg_r, 0)
```

Because if they are, then we could "separate" the operations from the conditions, and reduce the number of functions needed to handle the cases from A x B (pc_actions_OP_COND()) to A + B (pc_actions_COND(pc_actions_OP()).

Can you make the following changes:

1. Rename "_id" to "oid". Generally the leading underscore indicates things that a user should not change; thus, having a leading underscore variable be a parameter to a function is not really correct naming.
2. in the function signature, default oid to "None" (not ObjectId())
3. inside the function, you can add something like:
oid = oid or ObjectId()

This will set the default value to ObjectId() if it was originally None.

You might ask why not just set the default var to ObjectId() in the function header rather than the whole "None" business. The reason is because Python creates very terrible bugs if you put mutable objects inside function headers:

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1132941/least-astonishment-and-the-mutable-default-argument

That said, ObjectId() should be non-mutable and you are most likely OK even if you do not do 2 and 3 on the list above. But with this particular Python "feature/issue" of mutable default args I tend to be very cautious and only put things that are very clearly immutable as default vars.

Yep so because `airtime_amount` is an optional field, when it is not provided in your mutation it won't be passed to your `mutate` function and that is causing the error. Replace your `mutate` function def with:

```python
def mutate(root, info, msisdn, network_id, **kwargs):
	product_id = kwargs.get('product_id', None)
	airtime_amount = kwargs.get('airtime_amount', None)
```

The reason why it's not passed to your mutate function if it's not defined is so that you can differentiate between it being 'undefined' and it being null.

This is a design issue and not just a simple technical bug. Here's why:

If we have path/to/logdir/run1, path/to/logdir/run2, and you pass --logdir=path/to/logdir, you want the run names for run1 and run2 to be "run1" and "run2", naturally enough. But suppose there are events in path/to/logdir too. What should we call that run? Borrowing from unix conventions, we called it ".".

However, if you pass two unnamed logdirs, they both get auto-assigned "." and that creates the conflict.

Possibly we should fix this by requiring explicit names via the colon any time multiple logdirs are passed.

For completude, what we've found so far about this issue:

Matrix tests sometimes fails and there's no clear information about why.

To debug it, we added changes both to the synapse build and to the smoketest and pytest arguments to allow to run the tests in a self-running synapse server, with `--local-matrix=none`, as well as enabling the webclient to allow peaking in the rooms between test clients.

Sometimes a client fails completely to listen to events on a room it participates in, resulting in messages being sent by the sending party but not being handled by the receiving party. Code was added to ensure the listeners were properly added to the peers rooms, which fixed some errors, but not all. @pcppcp discovered that the `requests` session connection pool was being filled after long running rest sessions, because matrix http connections are long-polling connections which doesn't get closed immediately. He added a fix to increase the connection poll to 256, which seems to get rid of this problem, although we may look into ensuring proper socket close in the future.

Some of these errors have triggered not previously tested codepath, which showed hidden bugs in refund of mediated transfers #1627 #1653 .

Instead of introducing specific checks for all we can handle them by implementing the "Level One State Specific Replay Attack Mitigation" and "Level Two State Specific Replay Attack Mitigation" respectively.

> Well, if the network is under attack it can be benefitial, but, under normal operation I think it's a bad idea.

As said elsewhere: I believe we need to assess some actual attack vectors more closely. For day-to-day behavior we should be able to figure out some sane defaults for the throttling. 

The token bucket has two configuration dimensions:
- `capacity` of the bucket: this pretty much translates to the `burst` capacity of the throttled transport. So with the current `capacity` of `10`, any client is able to send `10` messages *completely unthrottled*. And after any `interval` (1s) without transfers any client will have the complete `burst` capacity available again.
- `refill_rate` of the bucket: this translates to the average maximum throughput over prolonged periods. With the current setting (`10`) any client is able to send up to `24 * 60 * 60 * 10 = 864000` messages per day.

Both of these values are just wild guesses at the moment. But I believe with this kind of throttling we (or a user) can find good values that don't interfere with a certain application, while also not leaving the client open to unlimited exploitation of the auto responding/forwarding behavior.

> Do you think you could also implement a per address throttle on the receiving of messages?

Sure, we can do that! I even think the same algorithm could be used for it, by maintaining one bucket per `sent_from` address. At this point I guess, we would want to use a different/lower `refill_rate`.

Due to differences in data types and models, there is certainly no lossless way to implement the save to file and load from another language feature. However, if we stick to a common data type, it is possible to exchange a subset of datatypes. This is more or less like a network protocol where the data type is pre-defined and the client can be implemented in any language. The obvious disadvantage of this approach is that the format cannot handle large and complex datatypes like dataframe.

The format of choice is obviously JSON, which is widely supported by many languages. I do not think it is wise to implement modules/libraries for different languages in SoS but we can keep track of JSON support in different languages.

* Python has [pyYAML module](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/PyYAML).
* R has [jsonlite](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/jsonlite/vignettes/json-aaquickstart.html)
* Matlab has [JSONlab](https://www.mathworks.com/matlabcentral/fileexchange/33381-jsonlab--a-toolbox-to-encode-decode-json-files?requestedDomain=www.mathworks.com)
This represent a major change on how sos scripts can be written. We should add a short tutorial on this.

I believe on MacOSX the docker image is running in a VM which is run in the user space so option `--user` does not matter outside, but will matter inside. The current behavior is good as it works with images with both root and non-root users. On Linux, allowing the default image user write to mounted drives is problematic because docker will either write root-owned files or cannot write at all, so a default non-root user makes more sense.

The decision is then if we should use `--user {getuid()}` only for Linux or for both systems...

Ok, the basic difference seems to be how long it takes to create an actor.

```python
import ray
ray.init()

@ray.remote
class Foo(object):
    def f(self):
        return 1

%timeit ray.get(Foo.remote().f.remote())  # 613ms in Python2, 1.12s with Python3.
```

One factor, which could partially account for this is the time required to start a Python interpreter.

```shell
# Python 2
> time python2 -c "print('hi')"  # 0.024s

# Python 3
> time python3 -c "print('hi')"  # 0.105s
```

Although that doesn't seem to explain the full difference.

I came across the removal of is_test recently. The optional input/output semantics has been cited as a reason for removing this flag. I don't think these semantics can be applied as-is in this case because it is not just about the number of outputs that the BatchNorm kernel creates; its about the behavior of the kernel that changes in training mode vs inference mode. The optional input/output semantics argument would apply if we're not going to support training at all. Moreover if we're not going to keep is_test, we should remove references to momentum, spatial and multiple outputs as well since they apply to training only and it only adds confusion to the spec.

I get a "YES" to "Are we willing to have VersionN to be able to handle Version1, Version2, ..., VersionN-1 model please?", at least we want to have the capability. This is what this proposal targets. I personally feel like it's a too big hammer, but I'm not against it that much as I don't see "the future" that clearly, Aha. More opinions may be heard from other folks :).

One more question based on this proposal please. Do we want to have a way to cut off the backward capability please? In this proposal, I'm assuming that ONNX is always backward compatible, because we have all the change logs since the beginning. It's up to the runtime (importers) to decide where it wants to start to support. By saying a cut off, we release some ONNX version, say 5.0, will be based on 3.0. The versions less than 3.0 are not supported.
